perturbation.original_text: Company shall comply with the provisions of the New York State Information Security Breach and Notification Act (General Business Law Section 889 -aa; State Technology Law Section 208).
perturbation.changed_text: Company shall acknowledge the existence of the New York State Information Security Breach and Notification Act.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'comply' to 'acknowledge', the contract removes the explicit requirement for the company to adhere to the security breach notification law, potentially leading to non-compliance and legal repercussions. The original text obligates the company to specific actions mandated by the act, while the modified text only requires acknowledgment of the law's existence without ensuring adherence, creating a legal contradiction and potential liability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York State Information Security Breach and Notification Act
perturbation.law_citation: General Business Law Section 899-aa; State Technology Law Section 208
perturbation.law_explanation: The New York State Information Security Breach and Notification Act mandates specific procedures and timelines for notifying affected individuals and state agencies in the event of a data breach. By weakening the compliance requirement to a mere acknowledgment, the modified text allows the company to potentially disregard these mandatory procedures, directly contradicting the law's intent and creating a risk of non-compliance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Bill Search and Legislative Information | New York State Assembly Skip to main content Javascript must be enabled to properly view this page. Bill No.: Summary Actions Committee&nbspVotes Floor&nbspVotes Memo Text LFIN Chamber&nbspVideo/Transcript A03870 Summary: BILL NO A03870 SAME AS SAME AS S02704 SPONSOR Jackson COSPNSR MLTSPNSR Amd §§364-m & 488, Soc Serv L; amd §2801, Pub Health L; amd §§31.02 & 32.05, Ment Hyg L Authorizes the commissioners of the department of health, the office of mental health, and the office of addiction services and support to jointly establish a single set of licensing standards and requirements for the construction, operation, reporting and surveillance of comprehensive outpatient services centers. Go to top A03870 Actions: BILL NO A03870 02/08/2023 referred to mental health 01/03/2024 referred to mental health Go to top A03870 Text: STATE OF NEW YORK ________________________________________________________________________ 3870 2023-2024 Regular Sessions IN ASSEMBLY February 8, 2023 ___________ Introduced by M. of A. JACKSON -- read once and referred to the Commit- tee on Mental Health AN ACT to amend the social services law, the public health law and the mental hygiene law, in relation to setting comprehensive outpatient services The People of the State of New York, represented in Senate and Assem- bly, do enact as follows: 1 Section 1. This act shall be known and may be cited as the "comprehen- 2 sive outpatient services act of 2023". 3 § 2. Section 364-m of the social services law is amended by adding a 4 new subdivision 6 to read as follows: 5 6. Comprehensive outpatient services centers. (a) Definitions. For 6 the purpose of this article, unless the context clearly requires other- 7 wise: 8 (i) "Mental health services" means services for the treatment of 9 mental illness. 10 (ii) "Addiction services" means services for the treatment of 11 addiction disorders. 12 (iii) "Comprehensive outpatient services" means the systematic coordi- 13 nation of evidence-based health care services, to include the preventa- 14 tive, diagnostic, therapeutic and rehabilitative care and treatment of 15 mental illness, addiction and the provision of physical health services, 16 otherwise provided by a diagnostic and treatment center or general 17 hospital outpatient program pursuant to article twenty-eight of the 18 public health law, a mental health clinic licensed pursuant to article 19 thirty-one of the mental hygiene law, or an addiction provider certified 20 pursuant to article thirty-two of the mental hygiene law to an individ- 21 ual seeking services regardless of their primary diagnosis or health 22 complaint; provided, however, that the scope of such services may be 23 restricted pursuant to regulation. EXPLANATION--Matter in italics (underscored) is new; matter in brackets [ ] is old law to be omitted. LBD07092-01-3 A. 3870 2 1 (iv) "Comprehensive outpatient services centers" means a facility 2 approved in accordance with this section to provide comprehensive outpa- 3 tient services in order to promote health and better outcomes for the 4 recipient, particularly for populations at risk. 5 (v) "Medical director" is a physician who is responsible for the 6 services delivered by the comprehensive outpatient services provider, 7 for the overall direction of the services provided and the direct super- 8 vision of medical staff in the delivery of services. 9 (vi) "Physical health services" means services provided by a physi- 10 cian, physician's assistant, nurse practitioner, or midwife acting with- 11 in his or her lawful scope of practice under title eight of the educa- 12 tion law and who is practicing in a primary care specialty. 13 (b) Notwithstanding any law, rule, or regulation to the contrary, the 14 commissioners of the department of health, the office of mental health, 15 and the office of addiction services and supports are authorized to 16 jointly establish a single set of licensing standards and r
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates compliance with the New York State Information Security Breach and Notification Act, while the changed text only requires acknowledgement of the law. This weakens the obligation significantly. The original requires specific actions mandated by the act, ensuring adherence, while the modified text does not, potentially leading to non-compliance. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to 'Data security protections' under 'ARTICLE 39-F Notification of Unauthorized Acquisition of Private Information', implying that compliance involves more than just acknowledging the existence of the law, but also implementing data security measures and notification protocols in case of a breach. The change thus removes the explicit requirement to adhere to the law, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Company shall be liable for the costs associated with such breach if caused by Company's negligent or willful acts or omissions, or the negligent or willful acts or omissions of Company's agents, officers, employees or subcontractors.
perturbation.changed_text: Company may be liable for some of the costs associated with such breach.
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the liability clause. New York law dictates that companies are responsible for costs resulting from negligence related to data breaches. The word 'may' introduces ambiguity, contradicting the legal obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York State Information Security Breach and Notification Act
perturbation.law_citation: General Business Law Section 899-aa; State Technology Law Section 208
perturbation.law_explanation: The New York State Information Security Breach and Notification Act holds businesses accountable for costs incurred due to data breaches resulting from their negligence. By replacing 'shall be liable' with 'may be liable', the modified text undermines this legal responsibility, potentially shielding the company from its mandated obligations under the Act.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text states that the company 'shall be liable' for costs associated with breaches caused by negligence, while the changed text states the company 'may be liable'. This introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation. The Law Explanation states that New York State Information Security Breach and Notification Act holds businesses accountable for costs incurred due to data breaches resulting from their negligence. The original text aligns with holding businesses accountable, whereas the changed text weakens this and contradicts this Act. The provided snippets do not directly address the specific clause, but the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both indicate a conflict with New York law regarding data breach liability.

perturbation.original_text: All laws governing the "Confidential Information" of the College, including FERPA - Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act, HIPPA - Health Insurance Portability Act, Gramm -Leach -Bliley Act, New York State Information Security Breach and Notification Act, Sarbanes -Oxley Act and others that may apply will be strictly followed.
perturbation.changed_text: The College expects the Company to be mindful of laws such as FERPA - Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act and HIPPA - Health Insurance Portability Act.
perturbation.explanation: The change removes the explicit requirement for strict adherence to all applicable laws, including data protection laws. This creates a legal contradiction because it eliminates the legally mandated obligation to comply with these laws. The original clause ensures strict adherence, while the modified version only expresses an expectation of mindfulness.
perturbation.contradicted_law: FERPA - Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act
perturbation.law_explanation: FERPA mandates the protection of student educational records. By removing the strict compliance clause, the contract creates the possibility of violating FERPA regulations, contradicting the Act's requirements for data protection.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states that all laws, including FERPA, HIPAA, and others, will be "strictly followed." The changed text removes this strict adherence requirement, replacing it with a mere expectation that the Company be "mindful" of laws like FERPA and HIPAA. This weakens the legal obligation to comply with these laws. The Law Explanation states that FERPA mandates the protection of student educational records. By removing the strict compliance clause, the contract creates the possibility of violating FERPA regulations. The scraped snippet 1 shows that the Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act of 1974 exists. This change directly contradicts the previous commitment to strict adherence, because it introduces a lower, subjective standard of compliance.

perturbation.original_text: The Company agrees that for Confidential Information it shall use the same degree of care and means it utilizes to protect its own information of a similar nature, but in any event not less than reasonable care and means, to prevent unauthorized use or disclosure of such Confidential Information to third parties.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company agrees that for Confidential Information it shall use reasonable care and means to prevent unauthorized use or disclosure of such Confidential Information to third parties.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the clause guaranteeing that the degree of care used for the college's confidential information would be *at least* as high as the degree of care used for the company's own information. Depending on the circumstances, this might violate New York's data security laws that implicitly mandate 'reasonable' security. The deletion weakens the level of protection and creates a potential legal contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: The New York SHIELD Act requires businesses to implement reasonable security measures to protect the private information of New York residents. By removing the guarantee of equivalent or higher care than the company uses for its own data, the modified text potentially falls short of the 'reasonable security' standard, creating a legal contradiction and increasing the risk of non-compliance with the SHIELD Act.
perturbation.justification: The original contract language ensured a minimum standard of care for confidential information, equivalent to or higher than the company's own data protection measures. The changed text removes this guarantee, potentially resulting in a lower standard of care, which may not meet the 'reasonable security' requirements under New York's data security laws. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that the modified text potentially falls short of the 'reasonable security' standard, increasing the risk of non-compliance with the SHIELD Act. The scraped snippet 1 refers to NYS Open Legislation, Article 39-F, which concerns data security protections, which supports the argument that the removal of the guarantee undermines the standard of care. This supports the argument that by removing the clause, it has made the contract legally contradictory.
